title: Add List Items
content:
### Adding Items to a List

---

### `.append()` -- Add at End

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
fruits.append("orange")
print(fruits)  # ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "orange"]
```

---

### `.insert()` -- Add at Specific Position

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
fruits.insert(1, "blueberry")
print(fruits)  # ["apple", "blueberry", "banana", "cherry"]
```

The first argument is the index, the second is the value.

---

### `.extend()` -- Add Multiple Items

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
more_fruits = ["orange", "kiwi"]
fruits.extend(more_fruits)
print(fruits)  # ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "orange", "kiwi"]
```

`.extend()` can add any iterable:

```python
fruits.extend(("grape", "melon"))  # Tuple
fruits.extend("Hi")  # String becomes ["H", "i"]
```

---

### The `+` Operator

```python
list1 = [1, 2, 3]
list2 = [4, 5, 6]
combined = list1 + list2
print(combined)  # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
```

---

> **Difference:** `append()` adds **one** element. `extend()` adds **each element** of an iterable individually. `+` creates a new list (does not modify either).
codeExample:
fruits = ["apple", "banana"]

fruits.append("orange")
print("After append:", fruits)

fruits.insert(1, "blueberry")
print("After insert:", fruits)

fruits.extend(["kiwi", "grape"])
print("After extend:", fruits)

new_list = [1, 2] + [3, 4]
print("Combined:", new_list)

quiz.question: Which method adds a single item to the end of a list?
